audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua Shechem covenant renewal, choose-this-day address, deaths, and burials, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for ancestral-god traditions, Abraham migration, Exodus memory, Balaam blessing, land-gift theology, choose-this-day confession, Joshua's warning against easy commitment, covenant stone witness, Joshua's death, Joseph's bones, and Eleazar's death.

verses.restored: Joshua gathered all tribes of Israel to Shechem and called elders of Israel, its heads, judges, and officers, and they stationed themselves before Divine.
Joshua said to all people, Thus says YHWH Divine of Israel: Across the River your fathers dwelt from old, Terah father of Abraham and father of Nahor, and they served other gods.
I took your father Abraham from across the River and led him through all land of Canaan, multiplied his seed, and gave him Isaac.
To Isaac I gave Jacob and Esau. To Esau I gave Mount Seir to possess, and Jacob and his sons went down to Egypt.
I sent Moses and Aaron, and I struck Egypt according to what I did in its midst; afterward I brought you out.
I brought your fathers out from Egypt, and you came to sea; Egyptians pursued your fathers with chariot and horsemen to Sea of Reeds.
They cried to YHWH, and he put darkness between you and Egyptians and brought sea upon him and covered him. Your eyes saw what I did in Egypt. Then you dwelt in wilderness many days.
I brought you to land of Amorite dwelling across Jordan; they fought with you, and I gave them into your hand. You possessed their land, and I destroyed them from before you.
Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, rose and fought against Israel; he sent and called Balaam son of Beor to curse you.
But I was unwilling to listen to Balaam; he blessed you indeed, and I rescued you from his hand.
You crossed Jordan and came to Jericho; lords of Jericho fought against you, Amorite, Perizzite, Canaanite, Hittite, Girgashite, Hivite, and Jebusite, and I gave them into your hand.
I sent before you the hornet/terror, and it drove them out from before you, two kings of Amorite; not by your sword and not by your bow.
I gave you land for which you did not labor and cities you did not build, and you dwell in them; vineyards and olive groves you did not plant, you are eating.
Now therefore fear/revere YHWH and serve him in wholeness and faithfulness; remove gods your fathers served across the River and in Egypt, and serve YHWH.

If it is evil in your eyes to serve YHWH, choose for yourselves this day whom you will serve: whether gods your fathers served across the River, or gods of Amorite in whose land you dwell. But as for me and my house, we will serve YHWH.
People answered and said, Far be it from us to forsake YHWH to serve other gods,

for YHWH our Divine is the one who brought us and our fathers up from land of Egypt, from house of slaves, and who did these great signs before our eyes and guarded us in all way we walked and among all peoples through whose midst we passed.
YHWH drove out all peoples and Amorite dwelling in land from before us. We also will serve YHWH, for he is our Divine.
Joshua said to people, You are not able to serve YHWH, for he is holy Divine; he is jealous Divine. He will not lift/carry your transgression and your sins.
If you forsake YHWH and serve foreign gods, then he will turn and do you harm and consume you after he has done good to you.
People said to Joshua, No, but YHWH we will serve.
Joshua said to people, Witnesses you are against yourselves that you have chosen YHWH for yourselves, to serve him. They said, Witnesses.
Now therefore remove foreign gods that are in your midst and incline your heart to YHWH Divine of Israel.
People said to Joshua, YHWH our Divine we will serve, and to his voice we will listen.
Joshua cut covenant with people on that day and set for them statute and judgment at Shechem.
Joshua wrote these words in scroll of Torah of Divine, and he took a great stone and set it up there under oak that was by sanctuary of YHWH.

Joshua said to all people, Look, this stone shall be witness against us, for it heard all sayings of YHWH that he spoke with us; it shall be witness against you, lest you deal falsely with your Divine.
Joshua sent people away, each man to his inheritance.
It was after these things that Joshua son of Nun, servant of YHWH, died, one hundred ten years old.
They buried him in territory of his inheritance, at Timnath-serah, in hill country of Ephraim, north of Mount Gaash.
Israel served YHWH all days of Joshua and all days of elders who lengthened days after Joshua and who knew all work of YHWH that he did for Israel.

Bones of Joseph, which children of Israel brought up from Egypt, they buried at Shechem, in portion of field Jacob bought from sons of Hamor father of Shechem for one hundred qesitah; and it became inheritance for sons of Joseph.
Eleazar son of Aaron died, and they buried him at Gibeah of Phinehas his son, which had been given to him in hill country of Ephraim.

translation_notes: Joshua's final covenant begins with a surprising memory: Israel's ancestors beyond the River served other gods. Covenant identity is narrated as a history of transformation, not ethnic spiritual purity.
The speech retells Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Esau, Egypt, Exodus, wilderness, Balaam, Jordan, Jericho, and land gift as one Divine first-person memory.
Esau's possession of Seir is acknowledged, preserving a parallel land-gift tradition rather than making Israel the only people with received territory.

The claim that Israel dwells in cities it did not build and eats vineyards and olive groves it did not plant is theologically central and ethically dangerous: gift language must not erase prior labor or inhabitants.
'Choose this day' is a covenant appeal to accountable service, not a manipulative altar-call formula or coercive demand for instant religious conformity.
Joshua resists easy commitment by warning the people that service is weighty; this prevents cheap vows but should not be used for spiritual intimidation.
The covenant stone is a witness object under the oak at Shechem, not an idol or magical surveillance device.
The deaths and burials of Joshua, Joseph's bones, and Eleazar close the book through grief, memory, and continuity rather than conquest alone.
safety_note: Do not use Joshua 24 to coerce conversion, shame ancestral religion, erase prior inhabitants' labor, demand instant vows through fear, or treat covenant identity as ethnic purity. Center honest memory, chosen responsibility, humility about inherited homes and fields, witness without surveillance, and grief-filled continuity after leaders die.
